questions.text: The dict has no eviction policy, so it grows unbounded for unique keys — is this intended for a fixed set of known keys, and if not, what is the expected memory ceiling or eviction strategy?

The implementation is documented as single-process only; if this is ever used behind a load balancer or multiple workers, state will diverge across processes — is there a plan to swap in a shared backend (e.g. Redis) later, or is single-process a permanent constraint?
Why is this placed under `examples/` rather than a library module or a dedicated `ratelimit/` package — is it intended purely as reference code, or will production paths import it directly?

answers.q1: Fixed set of known keys for this demo (a handful), so unbounded growth isn't a concern at this scale; a production version would add LRU/TTL eviction.
answers.q2: Single-process is a deliberate constraint for the example. A real deployment would swap in a shared backend (e.g. Redis) — intentionally out of scope here.
answers.q3: Purely reference code under examples/ — it signals "illustrative, not a supported library." Production paths do not import it.
